Beginner Guide

Beginner Gym Plan

Your complete 4-week guide to starting at the gym with confidence

Walking into a gym for the first time can be intimidating. The machines look complex, everyone seems to know what they're doing, and you're not sure where to start. This beginner gym plan solves all of that.

4 Weeks
Duration
3 Days
Days/Week
45-60 Min
Time/Session
Basic
Equipment

Week 1-2: Foundation Phase

Focus on learning proper form and building a routine. Weights should feel light to moderate.

Day 1: Full Body A

ExerciseSetsRepsRest
Goblet Squat310-1290s
Push-ups (or Knee Push-ups)38-1260s
Dumbbell Row310-1260s
Leg Curl Machine212-1560s
Plank320-30s60s

Day 2: Full Body B

ExerciseSetsRepsRest
Leg Press Machine312-1590s
Dumbbell Bench Press310-1290s
Lat Pulldown310-1260s
Dumbbell Shoulder Press210-1260s
Dead Bug310/side60s

Day 3: Full Body C

ExerciseSetsRepsRest
Romanian Deadlift (Dumbbells)310-1290s
Incline Dumbbell Press310-1290s
Seated Cable Row310-1260s
Lateral Raises212-1560s
Farmer&apos;s Walk330-40s60s

Week 3-4: Progression Phase

Increase weights by 5-10% when you can complete all sets with good form. Add one set to each exercise.

Progression Rules

  • ▸Add 2.5-5 lbs to upper body exercises when you hit the top rep range
  • ▸Add 5-10 lbs to lower body exercises when you hit the top rep range
  • ▸Never sacrifice form for weight - quality over quantity
  • ▸Rest 1-2 minutes between sets (3 minutes for heavy compound lifts)

Beginner Tips for Success

Start Light

It&apos;s better to start too light than too heavy. You have plenty of time to add weight.

Consistency Over Intensity

3 moderate workouts per week beats 1 brutal workout. Show up consistently.

Track Your Workouts

Write down weights, sets, and reps. Progressive overload requires knowing what you did last time.

Warm Up Properly

5-10 minutes of light cardio + dynamic stretching prevents injury and improves performance.

Prioritize Sleep

Muscles grow during rest, not during workouts. Aim for 7-9 hours per night.

Nutrition Matters

Protein intake of 0.7-1g per pound of bodyweight supports muscle growth and recovery.

Want a Personalized Plan?

Our AI creates customized workout plans based on your goals, experience level, and available equipment.
